sources.notes: Read the full current statement, original interaction, and hack format. It confirms n is a power of two with 4 <= n <= 2^30, the array has exactly one 1, 2 <= k <= n-1, the hidden state is fixed before interaction, and the per-test query limit is 33.

Read the official tutorial and accompanying code. They give equal-length cancellation followed by threshold binary search and the bound 2+1+29=32. The prose has a half/boundary wording slip; the official code queries the half without the 1, agreeing with the statement-derived condition that its length-n/2 answer is 1 exactly when k <= n/2.

Inspected the source and verified Codeforces verdict Accepted. This independent implementation uses two half queries plus two quarter queries to classify k into three ranges, then binary-searches with intervals whose membership bit is fixed; it uses at most 33 queries.

Re-derived Q(I) = [p in I] XOR [|I| >= k]. Exhaustively simulated every p and k for powers of two from 4 through 1024 and checked all boundary classes at n=2^30; the official variant always recovered k in at most 32 queries and the accepted alternative in at most 33.

annotation.summary: Recover a hidden threshold k from an interval oracle on a hidden one-hot binary array. The oracle returns whether the unique 1 lies in the interval, but flips that bit exactly when the interval length is at least k. The current hack format reveals p and k, while this review records the original interactive strategy.
annotation.constraints: Original interaction: 1 <= t <= 500; n=2^m with 2 <= m <= 30; the unique-1 position p is in [1,n]; 2 <= k <= n-1; p and k are fixed before interaction; at most 33 queries are allowed per test, and the final answer does not count. Current hacks use t <= 100 and supply n, p, and k explicitly.
annotation.core_idea: For an interval I, write Q(I)=[p in I] XOR [|I| >= k]. First query A=[1,n/4] and B=[n/4+1,n/2]. Their corruption bits are identical, so Q(A) XOR Q(B) cancels the unknown threshold effect and equals 1 exactly when p lies in the first half. This identifies the half H containing p and the opposite empty half E. Query E with length n/2: because its membership bit is 0, the reply is 1 exactly when k <= n/2. If k <= n/2, querying a length-x subinterval of E returns [x >= k]. If k > n/2, any anchored interval of length x >= n/2 that contains H also contains p and returns 1 exactly when x < k. Each case is therefore a monotone threshold search in x, so binary search returns k. Since k has n-2 possible values and each reply is one bit, any decision tree needs at least ceil(log2(n-2)) queries; the construction spends only a constant number more to neutralize the nuisance position p.
annotation.complexity: For n=2^m, the official strategy uses 2 quarter probes, 1 empty-half probe, and m-1 binary-search probes: m+2 <= 32 oracle queries, O(log n) local time, and O(1) memory. The alternative accepted decision tree uses at most m+3 <= 33 queries.
tricks: Compare equal-length queries so the unknown length-threshold flip cancels.
Use an interval known to be empty or guaranteed to contain p, fixing the membership bit and exposing a monotone predicate on interval length.
Treat the boundary inclusively: an empty interval of length n/2 returns 1 when k <= n/2, including k=n/2.
Reflect all intervals around the midpoint to handle p in the right half without duplicating the search logic.
Keep protocol separate from inference: print '? l r', flush, read one bit, terminate immediately on -1, and print '! k'; the answer line is not a query.

solution_variants.name: equal-length-cancellation-binary-search
solution_variants.summary: Query two adjacent first-half quarters to locate which half contains p by response xor. Query the opposite empty half at length n/2 to decide whether k is at most n/2. Binary-search k using variable-length intervals in the empty half for the small case, or variable-length intervals guaranteed to contain p for the large case; mirror coordinates when p is in the right half.
solution_variants.complexity: m+2 <= 32 oracle queries for n=2^m, O(log n) time, O(1) memory

solution_variants.name: four-probe-three-range-decision-tree
solution_variants.summary: Query both halves; their answers differ, and choose the half whose answer is 1. Query its two quarters. Quarter-answer sum 2 means that half is empty and k <= n/4; sum 0 means it is empty and n/4 < k <= n/2; sum 1 means it contains p and k > n/2. Binary-search the selected range with empty or guaranteed-containing anchored intervals.
solution_variants.complexity: At most m+3 <= 33 oracle queries for n=2^m, O(log n) time, O(1) memory

tags.evidence: The task reconstructs the hidden threshold k from adaptive one-bit interval-oracle replies while treating the hidden position p as a nuisance parameter and respecting a strict query budget.
The first two intervals have equal length, so xor-comparing their replies cancels the common threshold-corruption bit and reveals whether the unique 1 lies in their union, which is the first half.
After fixing the queried interval's membership bit to 0 or 1, the oracle response changes exactly at length x=k, making the final phase a monotone binary search for the first flipped length.
The accepted alternative uses four fixed probes whose response pattern selects one of three disjoint k ranges and the correct monotone oracle for the remaining search.
Reflecting interval endpoints maps the right-half case to the left-half case and keeps one binary-search implementation.
